coredata.dc:title: Platinum assisted by carbon quantum dots for methanol electro-oxidation
coredata.dc:creator: Pan, Dan
Li, Xingwei
Zhang, Aofeng
coredata.dc:description: Various types of fuel cells as clean and portable power sources show a great attraction, especially direct methanol fuel cell (DMFC) having high energy density, low operating temperature and convenient fuel storage. However, the preparation of low-cost Pt-based catalysts with satisfactory catalytic performance still faces many challenges for its commercialization on large scale. Here, Pt catalysts assisted by carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are reported. The synergistic effect of carbon quantum dots and Pt metals is similar to a bi-component catalyst, such as PtRu. First, carbon quantum dots derived from Vulcan XC-72 carbon black are synthesized by mixed acid etching. Then, carbon black (Vulcan XC-72) is soaked in carbon quantum dots solution for several days to obtain carbon black modified by carbon quantum dots (XC-72-CQDs). Finally, Pt catalysts are supported on XC-72-CQDs (Pt/XC-72-CQDs) through a simple chemical reduction method. For methanol electro-oxidation reaction, the catalytic performance of Pt/XC-72-CQDs is compared with commercial PtRu/C (30% Pt+15% Ru). Results show that a typical product (Pt/XC-72-CQDs5) exhibits a better catalytic activity than PtRu/C. In cyclic voltammetry test, the specific activity of Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 is 1.06mAcm−2 Pt and 477.6mAmg−1 Pt, while that of PtRu/C is 0.77mAcm−2 Pt and 280.6mAmg−1 Pt.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Platinum
Synergy
Water activation
Methanol oxidation

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful for the financial support from the financial support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51673064 ), and Shanghai Municipality Research Project ( 15520720500 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram of CQDs for activation of water and the oxidation of chemisorbed CO.
CV curves of Pt/XC-72-CQDs1, Pt/XC-72-CQDs3, Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 and Pt/XC-72-CQDs7 in 0.5M H2SO4 solution, with a scan rate of 50mVs−1.

(a) CV curves of Pt/XC-72-CQDs1, Pt/XC-72-CQDs3, Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 and Pt/XC-72-CQDs7 in 0.5M CH3OH and 0.5M H2SO4 with a scan rate of 50mVs−1; (b) CA curves of Pt/XC-72-CQDs1, Pt/XC-72-CQDs3, Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 and Pt/XC-72-CQDs7 in 0.5M CH3OH and 0.5M H2SO4 solution at a fixed potential of 0.15V (vs. Hg/Hg2SO4).
CV curves of Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 and PtRu/C in 0.5M H2SO4 solution, with a scan rate of 50mVs−1.

CV curves of PtRu/C and Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 in 0.5M CH3OH and 0.5M H2SO4 with a scan rate of 50mVs−1, area-normalized current densities (a) and mass-normalized current densities (c); CA curves of PtRu/C and Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 in 0.5M CH3OH and 0.5M H2SO4 with a fixed potential of 0.15V (vs. Hg/Hg2SO4), area-normalized current densities (b) and mass-normalized current densities (d).
Continuous ten times CA curves of PtRu/C (a) and Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 (b), and the decay curves of catalytic activity (c–d).
(a) TEM image of CQDs; (b) PL spectra of CQDs solution; (c) FTIR spectra of XC-72, XC-72-CQDs5 and CQDs; (d) the photographs of XC-72 carbon and XC-72/CQDs5 dispersed in de-ionized water for 7 days.
(a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of Pt/XC-72-CQDs5, the inset is the particle size distribution of Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 catalysts.
XRD patterns of PtRu/C and Pt/XC-72-CQDs5.
XPS core level spectra of C1s (a) and Pt 4f (b) for PtRu/C, C1s (c) and Pt 4f (d) for Pt/XC-72-CQDs5.
Catalytic performance of several Pt-based catalysts for methanol electro-oxidation.
Comparison of as-prepared catalysts for methanol electro-oxidation.
Results of the fits of the Pt 4f spectra, values gave in percentage of total intensity for PtRu/C and Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 catalysts.

head.ce:title: Platinum assisted by carbon quantum dots for methanol electro-oxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Advanced Polymeric Materials, Key Laboratory for Ultrafine Materials of Ministry of Education, School of Materials Science and Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Advanced Polymeric Materials
Key Laboratory for Ultrafine Materials of Ministry of Education
School of Materials Science and Engineering
East China University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Various types of fuel cells as clean and portable power sources show a great attraction, especially direct methanol fuel cell (DMFC) having high energy density, low operating temperature and convenient fuel storage. However, the preparation of low-cost Pt-based catalysts with satisfactory catalytic performance still faces many challenges for its commercialization on large scale. Here, Pt catalysts assisted by carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are reported. The synergistic effect of carbon quantum dots and Pt metals is similar to a bi-component catalyst, such as PtRu. First, carbon quantum dots derived from Vulcan XC-72 carbon black are synthesized by mixed acid etching. Then, carbon black (Vulcan XC-72) is soaked in carbon quantum dots solution for several days to obtain carbon black modified by carbon quantum dots (XC-72-CQDs). Finally, Pt catalysts are supported on XC-72-CQDs (Pt/XC-72-CQDs) through a simple chemical reduction method. For methanol electro-oxidation reaction, the catalytic performance of Pt/XC-72-CQDs is compared with commercial PtRu/C (30% Pt+15% Ru). Results show that a typical product (Pt/XC-72-CQDs5) exhibits a better catalytic activity than PtRu/C. In cyclic voltammetry test, the specific activity of Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 is 1.06mAcm−2 Pt and 477.6mAmg−1 Pt, while that of PtRu/C is 0.77mAcm−2 Pt and 280.6mAmg−1 Pt.

ce:section.ce:para:
Due to methanol easily stored, transported and handled characteristics, direct methanol fuel cell (DMFC) has been widely used for various fields [1–5]. DMFC having high energy density and low operating temperature is an effective clean energy, but the poor kinetic rate of methanol electro-oxidation reaction (MOR) is a serious defect hindering development of DMFC. A wide variety of nanostructured catalysts have been developed to solve above problem, including platinum-based catalysts [6–11] and non-platinum catalysts [12–16]. Pt-based catalysts play an important role in DMFC, which is an effective method to improve kinetic rate of methanol electro-oxidation reaction [17–19]. Several nanostructured Pt-based catalysts for methanol electro-oxidation are presented in Table 1
[20–27]. In Table 1, there are different types of supports for Pt-based catalysts, including meso/microporous carbons (MC), carbon nanofiber (CNF), graphene, multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWNT), nitrogen-doped carbon (N-dopedC) and their complexes with transition metals (Co-MWNT, Ru-CNF). In addition, there are commercial PtRu/C and Pt/C catalysts from Johnson Matthey Company (JM). More Pt-based catalysts supported on various supports were also summarized in detail by Huang et al. [28]. Although many Pt-based catalysts show good catalytic performance for MOR, Pt is a kind of costly noble metal, and its surface can be easily covered by intermediate carbonaceous species (mainly CO) [29,30]. Therefore, the development of low-cost Pt-based catalysts with excellent performances is always a crucial research in field of DMFC.

PtRu/C catalysts have also been proved to be efficient anode catalysts for MOR due to the bi-functional mechanism. In accordance with the double function mechanism, hydroxyl groups are generated on the surface of Ru under relatively low potential, hydroxyl groups react with carbon monoxide which is near Pt to promote the formation of carbon dioxide and obtain Pt with clean surface [31]. Ru atoms enhance the current efficiency of CO2 on Pt thus improving catalytic activity [32]. However, Ru as precious metal is also high cost and there is still much room for improvement. So far, a great deal of work has also been done to improve the performance of PtRu/C. For instance, Wang et al. [33] introduced commercial TiO2 into the preparation of PtRu/C catalysts to enhance the catalytic performance of PtRu/C. Liang et al. [34] obtained a composite carbon by blending multi-walled carbon nanotubes and carbon black, then PtRu alloy was supported on this composite. Moreover, Feng et al. [35] demonstrated that the performance of nanostructured PtRu catalysts could be improved by CoP material, the existence of CoP could retard the corrosion of Ru and Pt. Despite promising progress in the development of PtRu/C catalysts, the low activity and high cost of PtRu/C catalysts are still major obstacles for their applications in methanol electro-oxidation reaction.

As a new kind of nanomaterial in the carbon families, carbon quantum dots are widely used for chemical sensing, bio-imaging, drug delivery, photo-dynamic therapy, photo-catalysis and electric catalysis in recent years due to their high conductivity, good bio-compatibility and excellent physical and chemical properties [36–38].

Some studies have shown graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and CQDs can be employed for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) [39,40]. However, the contribution of CQDs for methanol electro-oxidation reaction has not been paid enough attentions. Recently, Hong et al. synthesized reduced graphene oxide modified by carbon quantum dots (CQDs/RGO), and used as the supports of Pt catalysts for methanol electro-oxidation. Results indicated that the catalytic performance of Pt-CQDs/RGO catalyst was superior to that of Pt/C and Pt/RGO [41]. Authors concluded that CQDs with abundant surface functional groups presented abundant nucleating and anchoring points to facilitate the formation of Pt catalysts with a small size and a uniform distribution. Above works reveal that CQDs, as a modified component, exhibit an obvious superiority to enhance performance of Pt catalysts for MOR, but the commercial carbon black (Vulcan XC-72) modified by CQDs as a support has currently not been discussed.

In this work, CQDs derived from Vulcan XC-72 carbon black are synthesized by mixed acid etching, and Vulcan XC-72 carbon black is soaked using CQDs solution for several days to obtain carbon black modified by CQDs (XC-72-CQDs). Then, Pt catalysts are supported on XC-72-CQDs supports through a simple chemical reduction method. The preparation process of Pt/XC-72-CQDs catalysts is depicted in Scheme 1 .
The catalytic performance of CQDs-assisted Pt is investigated for methanol electro-oxidation. For comparison, the catalytic performance of commercial PtRu/C (30% Pt +15%Ru) is also measured.

Vulcan XC-72 carbon black is supplied by Cabot and Akzonobel. H2PtCl6 and NaBH4 are purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Nafion® solution is purchased from Dupont. Commercial PtRu/C (30% Pt+15% Ru) catalysts are supplied by Johnson Matthey (JM). All other chemicals are of analytical grade.

0.3g of Vulcan XC-72 powder was dispersed in 30mL sulfuric acid (98%) and 10mL nitric acid (65%), and refluxed for 12h at 120°C. A bright yellow solution was obtained. After cooling down, the solution was diluted into 300mL, and the pH of above solution was adjusted to 7 with NaOH in an ice-bath. The product solution was further dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 1000Da) for 5days (replaced de-ionized water every 8h). Finally, CQDs powder was obtained by freeze-drying.

1g of XC-72 powder was soaked in 100mL de-ionized water contained 200mg of CQDs with ultrasonic and stirring treatment for 2h, then placed several days (1, 3, 5 and 7days) at a room temperature, respectively. Finally, these products were purged with de-ionized water several times, and dried at 60°C. A series of XC-72-CQDs supports were prepared, named XC-72-CQDs1, XC-72-CQDs3, XC-72-CQDs5 and XC-72-CQDs7. The content of CQD in above products was obtained by gravimetric analysis. The content of CQD was approximately 5.7%, 11.6% and 7.3% for XC-72-CQDs3, XC-72-CQDs5 and XC-72-CQDs7, respectively. The content of CQD for XC-72-CQDs1 could not be obtained accurately because it is too short to soak in carbon quantum dots solution.

Pt/XC-72-CQDs catalysts were synthesized by a chemical reduction method. A typical synthesis process was as follows: 30mg of XC-72-CQDs1 powder was dispersed into 5mL de-ionized water in ultrasonic cleaner for 20min, then 1mL 0.0386M H2PtCl6 solution was added under stirring. After that, 40mg of NaBH4 was slowly added with stirring for 3h under 55°C. Finally, the black powder was collected and dried under the vacuum conditions (60°C). Pt/XC-72-CQDs3, Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 and Pt/XC-72-CQDs7 were obtained through the same method.

The Luminous performance of CQDs was probed by UV–vis absorption spectra (UV/vis) (756S UV–vis spectrophotometer) and photoluminescence (PL) spectra (Fluorolog-3-P Fluorescence Spectrophotometer). And the structure of XC-72-CQDs was studied by Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) (Nicolet Magna-IR 550 spectrometer). The morphology of CQDs and Pt/XC-72-CQDs were characterized by a JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM). The crystal type of Pt catalysts was investigated by using D/max2550VB/PC (Rigaku Co. Japan) with wide-angle X-ray diffraction (XRD). The content of different valence state Pt was analyzed with X ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer).

CHI660D working station was used for electrochemical testing at a standard three-electrode cell. A glassy carbon electrode with a diameter of 3mm was served as a working electrode. The reference electrode was mercury sulfate electrode (Hg/Hg2SO4), and the counter electrode was a large-area glassy carbon (GC) electrode. The working electrode was obtained as follows: 2mg of catalysts were placed in 1mL of de-ionized and ultrasonically dispersed for 20min. After that, 10μL of above homogeneous suspension was transferred on a polished glassy carbon electrode, then dropping 5μL of 5wt.% Nafion ® solution to the surface of catalysts. After the solvent volatilized, the working electrode was obtained. Cyclic voltammograms (CV) and chronoamperometry (CA) tests were used to research the catalytic performance of as-prepared catalysts. CV curves were carried out in 0.5M CH3OH and 0.5M H2SO4 solution with a scan rate of 50mVs−1. CA curves were conducted by polarization at a fixed potential of 0.15V (vs. Hg/Hg2SO4) for 1000s (The maximum duration of CHI 660D is 1000s for CA measurement.). For comparison, the catalytic performance of commercial PtRu/C (30% Pt +15% Ru) was also measured under the same condition.

The catalytic performance of Pt/XC-72-CQDs1, Pt/XC-72-CQDs3, Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 and Pt/XC-72-CQDs7 catalysts are compared for methanol electro-oxidation reaction to select an appropriate support of XC-72-CQDs, as shown in Figs. 1 and 2
. The results from Figs. 1 and 2 are also summarized in Table 2 , including the forward peak current density (Jf) and potential (Ef), the backward peak current density(Jb) and potential (Eb), and the current density (JCA) after polarizing 1000s at a fixed potential of 0.15V (vs. Hg/Hg2SO4). Overall, Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 with a high CQDs content shows a better catalytic performance than other Pt/XC-72-CQDs catalysts. Therefore, Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 is selected as the representative of Pt/XC-72-CQDs catalysts to compare with commercial PtRu/C.

Electrochemical active surface area (EASA) is often used to normalize the current density of Pt catalysts. And the integral area of hydrogen adsorption/desorption peaks are served to assess EASA of Pt catalysts. Therefore, CV curves of Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 and PtRu/C catalysts are tested in 0.5M H2SO4 solution with a scan rate of 50mVs−1, as shown in Fig. 3 . EASA is calculated from the following equation [42,43]:
E A S A =
Q H
0.21 ×
M
P t
Where “QH” represents the electrical charge of hydrogen adsorption integrated from the CV curves, assuming that the surface of Pt is covered by monolayer hydrogen atom and the hydrogen adsorption electrical charge is 0.21mCcm−2 on clean surface of Pt. “MPt” is the loading of Pt on the working electrode. Based on the integrated areas of CV curves, EASA of Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 catalyst is estimated to be 44.96m2 g−1 Pt, which Pt loading is about 25% for Pt/XC-72-CQDs5, and that of commercial PtRu/C is 36.27m2 g−1 Pt.

Catalytic performance of as-prepared catalysts is measured in 0.5M CH3OH and 0.5M H2SO4 solution. In Fig. 4 a, there are two anodic peaks in the oxidation of methanol, representing two different states of Pt surface during the reaction. It is generally accepted that Pt is oxidized in the first forward scan, and then methanol is oxidized on the oxidized surface of Pt. In the reverse scan, first, Pt oxides are reduced, and then methanol oxidation occurs at a lower potential due to a clean, almost oxide-free surface of Pt catalysts [44,45]. Thus, the forward peak can represent the catalytic activity of methanol electro-oxidation reaction. Based on Fig. 4a and c, the area-normalized current densities of Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 and PtRu/C catalysts are 1.06 and 0.77mAcm−2 Pt, and the mass-normalized current densities are 477.6 and 280.6mAmg−1 Pt. However, the onset oxidation potential of Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 is higher than PtRu/C, implying that the adding of Ru may reduce the onset oxidation potential of methanol electro-oxidation.

The catalytic activity and stability of Pt catalysts are also studied by CA measurements, which is performed at a fixed potential of 0.15V (vs. Hg/Hg2SO4) in 0.5M CH3OH and 0.5M H2SO4 solution, as described in Fig. 4b and d. After 1000s, the area-normalized current densities of Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 and PtRu/C catalysts are 0.52 and 0.31mAcm−2 Pt, and the mass-normalized current densities are 232.3 and 110.7mAmg−1 Pt. This means that Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 has also good stability.

To further compare the durability of Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 and PtRu/C, the continuous ten times CA measurements are carried at a fixed potential of 0.15V (vs. Hg/Hg2SO4) in 0.5M CH3OH and 0.5M H2SO4, and results are given in Fig. 5 a–b. Fig. 5c–d shows that the decay of catalytic activity of Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 and PtRu/C, which are obtained from each CA measurement. After working 10000s, the specific activity of Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 is 0.39mAcm−2 Pt and 174.7mAmg−1 Pt, and PtRu/C catalyst is 0.20mAcm−2 Pt and 73.7mAmg−1 Pt.

On Pt catalysts, the hydrogen atoms in the methanol are stripped off through a series of reactions. This process can be written as.
(1) CH3OH+Pt=Pt-CH2OH+H+ +e−
(2) Pt-CH2OH=Pt-CHOH+H+ +e−
(3) Pt-CHOH=Pt-COH+H+ +e−
(4) Pt-COH=Pt-CO+H+ +e−
(5) Pt+H2O=Pt-OH+H+ +e−
(6) Pt-CO+Pt-OH=2Pt+CO2 +H+ +e−

During the methanol electro-oxidation, a methanol molecule removes four protons and be oxidized into carbon dioxide with releasing six electrons in this process. The released electrons transfer to the surface of catalysts and discharge finally. Above process shows that steps (1)–(4) take place on Pt sites, then Pt-CO is formed. Thus, the surface of Pt is enwraped with chemisorbed carbon monoxide during this period. Clearly, a key step in the process of methanol electro-oxidation is the conversion from the chemisorbed CO to CO2. Step 6 indicates that the further oxidation of chemisorbed CO to CO2 requires reaction with a hydroxyl group (OHads). Step (5) is the process of water activation and the formation of chemisorbed hydroxyl groups. Finally, chemisorbed OH reacts with chemisorbed CO to form carbon dioxide, which makes Pt surface clean, and then goes into the next catalytic cycle [45].

We speculate that Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 provides a rapid route for the activation of water. CQDs make possible the activation of water through the alternate step:
(7) CQDs+H2O=CQDs-OH+H+ +e− [46]

Subsequently, the additional reaction may be the following process.
(8) CQDs-OH+Pt-CO=CQDs+Pt+CO2 +H+ +e−
Thus, the activation process of water can proceed, even if the surface of Pt is covered by chemically adsorbed CO. The schematic diagram of above two processes is as follows: (Scheme 2 )

All results of catalytic performance indicate that CQDs should play an important role in enhancing the catalytic performance of Pt. This role might include the following three aspects: (1) CQDs with abundant oxygen-containing groups not only provide many active sites for the uniform dispersion of Pt catalysts, but also an interaction between Pt and CQDs improves the stability of Pt catalysts in the methanol electro-oxidation reaction; (2) CQDs can provide a rapid route for the activation of water, thus accelerate the conversion rate of chemisorbed CO to CO2; (3) CQDs with a good electronic conductivity can promote the transfer of electrons between Pt catalysts and methanol, and reduce the electrode resistance.

Why does Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 possess superior electrocatalytic activity and stability? For this purpose, the structure and morphology of CQDs and Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 are characterized. Fig. 6 a suggests that CQDs are highly dispersed. Fluorescence spectra of CQDs aqueous solution are showed in Fig. 6b, as the excitation wavelength increases from 300 to 500nm, the emission wavelength shifts from 475 to 560nm, revealing the excitation-dependent emission performance of CQDs. The illustrations in Fig. 6b gives the digital photos of CQDs aqueous solution and the resulting solution excited by a 365nm UV lamp. Obviously, the black powder becomes bright yellow transparent solution after chemical etching, and displays a bright kelly fluorescence under a 365nm UV light, which is consistent with the PL spectra [47,48].

The functional groups of XC-72 carbon black, XC-72-CQDs5 and CQDs are also investigated by FTIR spectra. In Fig. 6c, the peaks at 1620 and 1727cm−1 for CQDs are due to CO stretching vibration, while peaks at 3440 and 1110cm−1 are assigned to the bond of OH and CO [49]. These oxygen-containing groups make CQDs with excellent water solubility. Furthermore, the similar characteristic peaks, attributing to the oxygen-containing groups, can also be found in XC-72-CQDs5, but they have not appeared in XC-72 carbon black. This result shows that CQDs have successfully been modified to the surface of XC-72 carbon black. From Fig. 6d, it's not hard to find that XC-72-CQDs5 also possess good dispersion in aqueous solution. The water suspension of XC-72 carbon black quickly sinks, but that of XC-72-CQDs5 can steadily exist for 7days.

Fig. 7 a–b gives TEM and HRTEM of Pt/XC-72-CQDs5. The inset in Fig. 7a indicates that the diameter of Pt nanoparticles has a narrow distribution, range from 2.5 to 5.5nm with an average diameter of 4.0nm. In Fig. 7b, Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 shows a crystal lattice of 0.229nm, corresponding to the (111) crystal face of Pt [50,51]. Moreover, CQDs have also been observed on the surface of Vulcan XC-72.

Fig. 8 provides XRD patterns of Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 and PtRu/C. In XRD patterns, Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 catalyst shows an obvious wide diffraction peak of the graphite (002) planes at around 25.1°. Compared with Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 catalyst, the diffraction peak of Pt (200) has obvious change for PtRu/C due to the formation of PtRu alloy. For Pt/XC-72-CQDs5, the diffraction peaks at about 39.8°, 46.3°, 67.7°, 81.5° and 85.9° are corresponding to Pt (111), Pt (200), Pt (220), Pt (311) and Pt (222) characteristic diffraction [52,53]. It is also easy to find that the diffraction peak of Pt (222) in PtRu/C is too weak to identify, but this peak becomes strong in Pt/XC-72-CQDs5. Perhaps, this difference is also beneficial for improving the catalytic performance of Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 [50,54].

The analysis of element compositions and valences are characterized by XPS spectra and are shown in Fig. 9 . As depicted in Fig. 9a, c, there are three well resolved peaks in spectrum of C1s which are ascribed to the following carbon functional groups: CC/CC (∼284.6eV), COH (∼285.4eV) and CO (∼287.8eV). In Fig. 9b and d, three valence states of Pt can be identified according to doublet binding energy of Pt4f7/2 and Pt4f5/2 for as-prepared catalysts. The content of various valence states Pt can be calculated according to the respective peak area. The ratio and binding energy of different valence states Pt are shown in Table 3 . For PtRu/C, the atomic ratio of Pt (0)/Pt (II)/Pt (IV) is ∼27/21/52, while for Pt/XC-72-CQDs5, this ratio is ∼60/21/19. Obviously, Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 has higher content of Pt (0) and lower content of Pt (IV) than PtRu/C. Some researchers believe that Pt (0) is beneficial for the adsorption of methanol at the active site [55] and the corrosion resistance of Pt (0) can provide good durability [56,57]. In addition, it has been accepted that Pt (II) component can promote COads into CO2 [55], and has also been considered to be active for MOR [58,59].

In this work, CQDs is adopted to enhance the performance of Pt catalysts. Compared with commercial PtRu/C, as-prepared Pt/XC-72-CQDs5 catalyst exhibits superior electrocatalytic activity and stability for the methanol electro-oxidation reaction. Here, the synergistic effect of CQDs and Pt similar to a bi-component catalyst. CQDs can provide a rapid route for the activation of water, which is favorable to accelerate the oxidation of chemisorbed CO to CO2.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial support from the financial support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51673064), and Shanghai Municipality Research Project (15520720500).
